text: Originally documented by Brussee and co-workers almost three decades ago, skeletal editing (SE) as a contemporary synthesis tool has only recently come of age as a formal strategy in contemporary synthesis. As the individual practice of manipulating a molecule at the level of individual atoms—regardless of the method used to make these alterations—this review surveys recent advancements in single-atom insertion, edit, and substitution operations in cyclic systems, including heterocycles. By focusing on recent advancements in these innovative approaches and describing the thinking that has evolved in the synthesis community as late-stage skeletal logic has increasingly begun to enter practice, we detail how late-stage skeletal logic has started to redefine contemporary strategies for design and structural diversity in modern chemistry.
